data.title: Stem Inc.: Deciphering the SaaS Pivot and Strategic Realignment
data.description:
The global energy landscape is currently undergoing a structural shift from centralized, fossil-fuel-dependent generation to a decentralized, intermittent, and digitally orchestrated architecture. At the center of this transition lies the critical need for an intelligent orchestration layer, software capable of managing the inherent volatility of renewable energy assets while maximizing their economic utility. Stem Inc. has emerged as a vanguard in this niche, having recently executed a profound strategic pivot from a hardware-intensive business model to a high-margin, software-centric enterprise. This transformation, initiated in late 2024 and solidified through 2025, represents more than a tactical shift; it is a fundamental reconfiguration of the company's capital allocation and operational execution designed to surface intrinsic value that remains obscured by legacy market perceptions.

A Core Investment Thesis

The foundational thesis for investing in Stem Inc. rests on two distinct engines of value creation: a compelling steady-state Free Cash Flow (FCF) yield for the outright owner, and a high-probability "multiple re-rating" as the market recognizes the shift from hardware reseller to AI software provider.

Why the Stock is Mispriced

Stem is suffering from a "hardware hangover." Historically, the market valued Stem as a lumpy, low-margin battery distributor. Today, the market continues to price the equity as a distressed industrial firm with a trailing twelve-month (TTM) P/E of approximately 1.5xa figure mathematically skewed by one-time debt restructuring gains but indicative of the extreme disconnect from the US Electrical industry average of 32.9x.

The Path to 2x Returns

A 100% return within the next 12 months is supported by the convergence of these two engines:

The Economic Engine: At current prices, an owner is buying a stabilized software business that generates a double-digit FCF yield on equity.

The Sentiment Engine: As Stem demonstrates four consecutive quarters of positive Adjusted EBITDA (of which it has already achieved two), the market is likely to shift its valuation framework from a distressed 0.9x P/S multiple toward a conservative 3-4x SaaS/Infrastructure software multiple.

The Outright Owner's Payoff: FCF Yield and Capital Torque

For a permanent owner, the relevant starting point is the Enterprise Value (EV), which accounts for the total cost to acquire the business including its debt burden.

Enterprise Value Implication

As of early 2026, Stem's market capitalization sits at approximately $156.6 million. Following its transformational June 2025 restructuring, the balance sheet reflects a significant but extended debt profile.

Total Debt: Approximately $342 million (consisting of $155 million in new 1st Lien Notes and ~$187 million in remaining convertible notes).

Cash Position: ~$43.1 million.

Implied EV: $455.5 million.

At an EV of ~$455 million, an owner is paying for an Annual Recurring Revenue (ARR) base of $60.2 million that carries 80% software gross margins.

Steady-State FCF and Owner Yield

To determine if Stem stands on its own economically, we model the cash flow available to a 100% owner after accounting for the "New Stem" cost structure:

Gross Profit Potential: The current $60M ARR @ 80% margin plus ~$80M in managed services/edge hardware @ ~35% blended margins suggests an annual gross profit capability of ~$76 million.

Cash OpEx Discipline: Management reduced cash operating expenses by 47% YoY through 2025.11 Stabilized OpEx is estimated at ~$35 million/year.

The Debt Burden: The 11% cash interest on the new $155M notes (~$17M) plus interest on legacy notes (~$5M) creates a ~$22 million annual cash interest obligation.

Steady-State Payoff Matrix:

Even if growth stalls today, the owner is being "paid" a 12% yield in implied cash generation. At the current 17% ARR growth rate, the implied FCF yield of 20%+ suggests the equity is drastically undervalued purely on its unit economics.

The Torque of Leverage

The 11-12% interest rate is a "red flag" for many, but for the owner, it provides massive capital torque. Because the market cap ($156M) is small relative to the debt ($342M), any incremental growth in EV flows disproportionately to the equity. For example, a $100M increase in Enterprise Value (driven by EBITDA expansion) would result in a ~64% increase in the share price, holding debt constant.

Near-Term Catalyst: The European Hub and PowerTrack EMS

Value realization in 2026 will be driven by the ramp-up of Stem's Berlin competence center, announced in October 2025. This hub targets complex technical challenges like merchant market risk and negative pricing in Europe, high-value software use cases that allow Stem to charge premium SaaS fees. Furthermore, the launch of PowerTrack EMS targets the utility-scale hybrid market, expanding the total addressable market (TAM) as standalone solar and storage projects converge.

Long-Term Outlook: The AI-Driven Data Center Symbiosis

Over a multi-year horizon, Stem is positioned to benefit from the data center industry's demand for 24/7 carbon-free energy. Major power purchase agreements (PPAs), such as the 2,600 MW deal between Vistra and Meta, highlight the need for firm, reliable clean energy. Stem's leadership in optimizing behind-the-meter assets for commercial and industrial (C&I) customers makes it an essential partner for infrastructure providers who must employ massive battery storage systems optimized by AI to meet "net-zero" goals while maintaining 99.9% uptime.

Anatomy of the Bear Case: Leverage and Execution Risk

The primary risk to the owner is the 11-12% cost of debt. While the "Pay-in-Kind" (PIK) toggle provides a liquidity safety valve, it is a high hurdle for a company with a negative book value. If ARR growth stalls below 10%, the principal balance could swell faster than the cash flow can service it, potentially leading to a 2030 restructuring that wipes out equity holders. Additionally, vertically integrated competitors like Tesla (Autobidder) remain a constant threat to Stem's third-party software model in the utility-scale segment.

Institutional Sentiment: Tracking the Flow of Smart Money

Despite the volatility, institutional investors have shown increasing interest in Stem's turnaround. As of late 2025, 119 institutional owners held approximately 25% of the shares, with total institutional value reaching $34 million. Major holders like Vanguard Group and Bank of America have significantly increased their positions, with Bank of America raising its stake by 104% in late 2025. High-profile hedge funds such as Marshall Wace and Point72 have also entered the fray, with Marshall Wace taking a new position of over 243,000 shares in September 2025.

Short interest has also seen a gradual decline, falling from nearly 27% in early 2025 to 13.85% by December 31, 2025. This "short covering" suggests that the market's most bearish participants are reassessing the risk-reward profile following the debt exchange and the achievement of positive operating cash flow. While the stock remains volatile, the concentration of holdings among sophisticated global asset managers provides a level of technical support that was absent during the downward spiral of 2023.

The Valuation Anchor: Intrinsic Value and the Multi-Multiple Gap

The current valuation of Stem Inc. presents a compelling case of statistical arbitrage. To understand the "Intrinsic Value" of the enterprise, one must look at the divergence between current multiples and historical averages, as well as peer-group comparisons. Stem's latest twelve-month (LTM) P/E ratio of 1.5x is a profound anomaly. Historically, over the last five years, Stem has operated at a median P/E ratio of -11.2x, as the company was in a heavy loss-making, high-growth phase. The shift to a positive P/E, even if influenced by one-time restructuring gains, signals a fundamental change in the business's earnings power.

When comparing Stem to its peers, the undervaluation becomes even more stark. Fluence Energy (FLNC) and Enphase Energy (ENPH) command significantly higher multiples of revenue and earnings due to their perceived market leadership and operational stability. Enphase, for instance, has a 5-year average P/E of approximately 100.8x, though its current multiple has moderated to 27.35x as the solar market faces cyclical headwinds. Stem's forward P/E of approximately 2.0x (based on consensus estimates of future earnings) is not just below the industry; it is essentially pricing the company as if it were a declining commodity business rather than a high-margin AI software player.

Conclusion: The Investment Verdict on Stem Inc.

Stem Inc. represents a rare opportunity where the "Hard Economic Engine" (an implied 12-20% FCF yield) and the "Sentiment Engine" (a massive valuation gap vs. peers) are both pointed upward. For the long-term owner, the current price offers a margin of safety rooted in high-margin recurring revenue. The convergence of software margin expansion, institutional inflow, and secular tailwinds from data centers suggests that a 2x outcome is not a speculative dream, but a baseline expectation for a firm finally surfacing its true intrinsic value.

data.title: ICE Canada Morning Comment: ICE Canola at 7-Month Highs, Still Rising With Oil
data.description:
WINNIPEG, Manitoba--ICE canola futures were stronger Thursday morning, hitting their highest levels in seven months.

The Iran conflict kept crude oil values climbing higher Thursday, with that strength spilling into the vegetable oil markets.

Gains in Chicago soyoil, Malaysian palm oil and European rapeseed futures were all contributing to the firmer tone in canola.

Statistics Canada released planting intentions data Thursday morning, estimating a 1.1% increase in canola area on the year at 21.8 million acres. That was below average trade estimates, with most market participants expecting actual area to end up above 22 million acres.

Bullish chart signals contributed to the gains, although the market was starting to look overbought by some technical metrics.

About 24,600 canola contracts had traded as of 9:58 a.m. ET.

Prices in Canadian dollars per metric tonne at 9:58 a.m. ET:

Canola 
           Price      Change 
May       712.60     up 3.20 
Jul       722.90     up 3.00 
Nov       713.60     up 2.70 
Jan       720.20     up 2.80

Source: Commodity News Service Canada, news@marketsfarm.com

data.title: Global Ship Lease Inc reports results for the quarter ended December 31 - Earnings Summary
data.description:
• Global Ship Lease Inc reported quarterly adjusted earnings of $2.32​​ per share for the quarter ended December 31, lower than the same quarter last year, when the company reported EPS of $2.55. The mean expectation of two analysts for the quarter was for earnings of $2.45 per share. Wall Street expected results to range from $2.31 to $2.58 per share.

  • Revenue rose 4.7% to $190.95 million from a year ago; analysts expected $185.00 million.

  • Global Ship Lease Inc's reported EPS for the quarter was $2.79​.

  • The company reported quarterly net income of $100.22 million.

  • Global Ship Lease Inc shares had risen by 14.8% this quarter.

FORECAST CHANGES

  • The mean earnings estimate of analysts had risen by about 1.8% in the last three months.​

  • In the last 30 days, there have been no earnings estimate revisions by analysts covering the company.

RECOMMENDATIONS

  • The current average analyst rating on the shares is "hold" and the breakdown of recommendations is 1 "strong buy" or "buy," 2 "hold" and no "sell" or "strong sell." The average consensus recommendation for the marine freight & logistics peer group is "buy."

  • Wall Street's median 12-month price target for Global Ship Lease Inc is $37.00, about 8.7% below its last closing price of $40.22

This summary was machine generated from LSEG data March 5 at 03:25 p.m. UTC. ​All figures in US dollars unless otherwise stated. data.title: U.S. Import Prices Rise 0.2% In January, Slightly More Than Expected
data.description:
WASHINGTON (dpa-AFX) - A report released by the Labor Department on Thursday showed import prices in the U.S. increased by slightly more than expected in the month of January.

The Labor Department said import prices rose by 0.2 percent in January, matching an upwardly revised uptick in December. Economists had expected import prices to inch up by 0.1 percent.

The slightly stronger than expected import price growth came as an increase in prices for non-fuel imports more than offset an extended slump in prices for fuel imports.

The report said prices for non-fuel imports climbed by 0.5 percent in January after rising by 0.2 percent in December, while prices for fuel imports tumbled by 2.2 percent in January after slumping by 1.1 percent in December.

The increase in prices for non-fuel imports reflected higher prices for non-fuel industrial supplies and materials, capital goods, automotive vehicles, consumer goods and foods, feeds, and beverages.

The Labor Department also said import prices edged down by 0.1 percent year-over-year in January after coming in unchanged in December.

'Risks are tilted toward higher import prices in 2026 thanks to higher global oil prices, strong demand for capital goods imports, and past depreciation in the dollar,' said Grace Zwemmer, U.S. Economist at Oxford Economics.

She added, 'Despite that risk, we still expect core inflation to moderate as 2026 unfolds, allowing the Federal Reserve to lower interest rates in June and September.'

Meanwhile, the report said export prices climbed by 0.6 percent in January, which also matched an upwardly revised increase in December. Economists had expected export prices to rise by 0.2 percent.

Prices for agricultural exports rose by 0.2 percent in January after coming in unchanged in December, while prices for non-agricultural exports increased by 0.7 percent for the second straight month.

The Labor Department said the annual rate of export price growth slowed to 2.6 percent in January from 3.1 percent in December.

Copyright RTT News/dpa-AFX

data.title: US allows transactions with Rosneft Germany despite Russia sanctions
data.description:
The United States on Thursday issued a general license exempting transactions involving the German unit of Russia's Rosneft from U.S. sanctions, according to the U.S. Treasury Department website.

The license, which does not include an end date, replaces one that was set to expire on April 29.

The extension of the existing U.S. sanctions ​waiver ​reduces the risk ⁠of disruptions to German refining operations at a time when the escalating ​conflict in the Middle East is unsettling ​global ⁠energy markets.

Germany placed the local units of Russia's ​Rosneft under trusteeship in 2022 after Moscow's invasion of Ukraine upended Berlin's decades-long energy ties with Russia.

The assets include a ​stake in the PCK Schwedt refinery, a key ​supplier of fuel to the capital region.

Bloomberg News, citing a person familiar with the matter, reported on Wednesday that the U.S. government would exempt Rosneft Germany from sanctions indefinitely.

data.title: LatAm assets decline as Middle East war concerns linger
data.description:
Key points:
  • Stocks drop 2.7%, FX down 0.8%
  • Data shows Brazil jobless rate rose 5.4% in three months Peruvian equities, Mexican peso lead regional losses
  • Most Asian, emerging Europe markets recover from sharp sell-off

By Twesha Dikshit

LatAm assets pulled back on Thursday with an escalating war in the Middle East and continued shutdown of the Strait of Hormuz weighing on investor risk sentiment, while oil prices surged higher due to supply concerns.

The U.S.-Israeli war against Iran entered its sixth day and showed no signs of abating with Azerbaijan warning it was preparing a response to two Iranian drones flying across its border and wounding people. Iran has denied the attack.

The conflict has disrupted oil supplies with nine vessels coming under attack since it began. Many gas and oil exporters in the region halted production with Iran targeting energy infrastructure.

While regional impact in EM will differ with analysts pointing to oil exporters being more vulnerable to a prolonged escalation, concerns over the impact on global inflation remain with positions in risky EM assets likely to be reduced first.

"In a prolonged risk‑off environment with a stronger dollar and higher oil prices, all EM assets are vulnerable," said Elias Hilmer, markets economist at Capital Economics.

"EMEA appears most exposed due to proximity to the conflict and partly weaker balance sheets, while LatAm and Asia may hold up better thanks to lower reliance on energy imports and lower equity valuations in LatAm, and stronger balance sheets in Asia."

The MSCI Latin American equity index (.MILA00000PUS) dropped 2.7% after recovering some of its losses in the previous session. A corresponding currency gauge (.MILA00000CUS) was down 0.7% against a stronger dollar.

EM REGIONAL MARKET IMPACT DIFFERS

Brazil's stocks edged down 1.9%. Official data showed the country's jobless rate rose to 5.4% in the three months through January. The real lost 0.5% against the greenback.

Central bank monetary policy director Nilton David said the organization would take a measured approach in assessing data with the Middle East conflict driving oil prices higher, potentially fuelling inflation.

Brazil's rate-setting committee, which will meet on March 17-18, had signaled the interest-rate-lowering cycle to begin in March but developing geopolitical risks could alter expectations.

Benchmark indexes in copper exporter Peru (.MXNUAMPESCPGPE) and Chile , reliant on oil imports, dropped 1.7% each while the Chilean peso weakened 1.6%.

Mexico's equities fell 1.8% while its peso edged down 0.6%.

In the wider EM universe, equities in Romania , Hungary and Iran-neighbor Turkey gained over 1% each, recovering from some of this week's steep losses.

Middle Eastern bourses were mostly higher, with exchanges in Dubai, UAE and Bahrain serving as outliers.

Asian markets recovered from the previous session's rout with Korea's blue-chip index closing up 9.6%, while those in Thailand , Taiwan TWSE:TAIEX and Indonesia adding between 1.8% and 2.6%.

data.title: Amazon Launches Agentic AI Service to Reduce Administrative Burdens in Healthcare
data.description:
By Elias Schisgall

Amazon Web Services is launching Amazon Connect Health, an agentic artificial-intelligence service aimed at cutting down on administrative burdens in healthcare.

The AI agent will be integrated with healthcare providers' electronic health records and will be able to review medical histories, check insurance, take notes, and schedule appointments, Amazon Senior Vice President Colleen Aubrey wrote in a Thursday blog post.

Connect Health was built using AWS's Connect, the company's artificial intelligence customer service solution.

Amazon One Medical, Amazon's primary healthcare service, has led deployment of Amazon Connect Health in clinical settings, the company said. It has also partnered with UC San Diego Health and Netsmart, a healthcare technology company.

Early deployments have cut down on phone call times, reduced call abandonment rates, and raised ambient documentation adoption.

Earlier this year, One Medical launched Health AI, an agentic AI assistant for patients that can give health advice, explain lab results, manage medications, and book appointments.

Write to Elias Schisgall at elias.schisgall@wsj.com

data.title: Revolut seeks de novo banking charter with OCC
data.description:
Revolut is the latest crypto-friendly firm to apply for a U.S. banking charter with the Office of the Comptroller of the Currency (OCC) and the Federal Deposit Insurance Corporation (FDIC), the Financial Times was first to report on Thursday.

The filing comes shortly after Revolut dropped plans in January to acquire a U.S. lender and instead sought a de novo banking license, given the OCC's accelerated approval pathway that has already given conditional approval to several stablecoin issuers to become banks.

In November, Revolut closed a share sale at a $75 billion valuation, a 66% increase from its $45 billion valuation in 2024.

The move comes amid a sea change in banking oversight in the U.S. Perhaps most notably, on Wednesday, Kraken became the first crypto native firm to receive a "skinny" master account with the Federal Reserve, giving it direct access to core U.S. payment systems.

According to FT, there were 14 OCC de novo banking applications last year, "almost as many as the total for the preceding four years" under the decidedly more hardline Biden administration.

Revolut has previously said expanding in the U.S. market is core to its growth strategy. "This is a major milestone in our mission to build the world’s first truly global bank," the company posted to X on Thursday.

The firm drafted a preliminary application for the FDIC and California regulators in 2021, without formally submitting. Its current banking services are provided by Lead Bank, Revolut said.

While Revolut’s statement doesn't mention stablecoins directly, a banking charter could help the firm expand its stablecoin offerings in the U.S. under the guidance of the GENIUS Act, which provided a framework for stablecoin businesses when enacted in July 2025.

In February, the Financial Conduct Authority selected Revolut to join other participants in a fiat-pegged stablecoin sandbox payments trial.

data.title: U.S. Jobless Claims Unchanged At Slightly Upwardly Revised Level
data.description:
WASHINGTON (dpa-AFX) - A day ahead of the release of the more closely watched monthly jobs report, the Labor Department released a report on Thursday showing first-time claims for U.S. unemployment benefits came in flat in the week ended February 28th.

The Labor Department said initial jobless claims came in at 213,000, unchanged from the previous week's revised level.

Economists had expected jobless claims to inch up to 215,000 from the 212,000 originally reported for the previous week.

'Initial jobless claims are consistent with our view that labor-market conditions have stabilized and will improve as 2026 unfolds,' said Nancy Vanden Houten, Lead U.S. Economist at Oxford Economics.

The report said the less volatile four-week moving average fell to 215,750, a decrease of 4,750 from the previous week's revised average of 220,500.

Meanwhile, the Labor Department said continuing claims, a reading on the number of people receiving ongoing unemployment assistance, increased by 46,000 to 1.868 million in the week ended February 21st.

The four-week moving average of continuing claims also rose to 1,851,500, an increase of 6,750 from the previous week's revised average of 1,844,750.

'Continued claims jumped in the week ended February 21, but continued claims tend to be more volatile week to week, and we expect to see at least some of the increase reversed in the week ended February 28,' said Vanden Houten.

On Friday, the Labor Department is scheduled to release its more closely watched report on employment in the month of February.

Economists currently expect employment to rise by 60,000 jobs in February after jumping by 130,000 jobs in January, while the unemployment rate is expected to inch up to 4.4 percent from 4.3 percent.
